card.title: What CBT Actually Does
card.teaser: It's the most-researched therapy on Earth — and the trick at its core is almost absurdly simple.
card.coverImagePrompt: A person in a softly lit therapy room, thought bubbles above their head being gently rearranged by their own hand, warm painterly style, introspective mood

lessons.title: A therapist's strange question
lessons.body: You tell your therapist you're convinced you'll fail the presentation. Instead of asking how you feel, she asks something weird: 'What's the evidence?' You pause. You realize you've nailed the last three. Something shifts. That tiny move is the entire engine of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy.

visual.imagePrompt: Two people in a sunlit therapy office, warm natural lighting, quiet moment of reflection

lessons.title: The triangle that runs your life
lessons.body: CBT is built on one idea: thoughts, feelings, and behaviors form a triangle, and each corner pulls on the others. A thought like 'I'm going to mess this up' creates anxiety, which makes you avoid practicing, which makes you actually mess up — confirming the thought. The loop closes. CBT looks for where to cut it.

keyTerms.definition: The interaction between thoughts, feelings, and behaviors
A systematic thinking error that warps reality

visual.imagePrompt: A glowing triangle with 'Thoughts,' 'Feelings,' and 'Behaviors' at its corners, arrows circulating between them

lessons.title: Catching the lies your brain tells
lessons.body: Aaron Beck, a 1960s psychiatrist, noticed his depressed patients had thought patterns that weren't just sad — they were wrong. Catastrophizing ('this is a disaster'), mind-reading ('she hates me'), black-and-white thinking ('I'm a total failure'). CBT trains you to catch these distortions, write them down, and ask the therapist's magic question: what's the actual evidence?

visual.imagePrompt: A journal page with handwritten thoughts, some crossed out and replaced with more balanced versions, pen resting beside it

lessons.title: Why it keeps winning trials
lessons.body: Hundreds of randomized controlled trials have put CBT up against other therapies, medication, and placebo. For depression, anxiety, insomnia, and PTSD, CBT consistently matches or outperforms antidepressants — and the effects last longer after treatment ends. A 2018 meta-analysis of over 400 studies found CBT worked across cultures, age groups, and delivery formats, including online.
lessons.funFact: A single CBT course typically runs 8 to 20 sessions — far shorter than classical psychoanalysis, which could last for years.

visual.imagePrompt: Abstract visualization of mood recovery curves rising over weeks, scientific chart aesthetic

lessons.title: You can steal the tools
lessons.body: You don't need a therapist to try the core move. Next time you feel lousy, write down the exact thought behind it. Ask: is this a fact or a feeling dressed as one? What would I tell a friend who said this? That's it. That's the whole trick. Repeat it a few thousand times and you've rewired a habit. That's CBT in the wild.

quiz.question: Which three elements form the CBT cognitive triangle?
quiz.explanation: CBT treats thoughts, feelings, and behaviors as a loop, each one influencing the others.

quiz.question: Who is most associated with founding modern CBT?
quiz.explanation: Aaron Beck developed cognitive therapy in the 1960s after noticing consistent thought patterns in depressed patients.

quiz.question: What's a 'cognitive distortion'?
options: A brain injury that warps vision
A systematic thinking error that misrepresents reality
quiz.explanation: Cognitive distortions are predictable thinking errors like catastrophizing or mind-reading.

sources.title: Cognitive Therapy of Depression
sources.publisher: Aaron T. Beck et al., Guilford Press

sources.title: The Efficacy of Cognitive Behavioral Therapy: A Review of Meta-analyses
